title: Liquidity in Plain Terms: Why It Decides Everything in DeFi
description: On a traditional exchange, the price is set by the order book: some want to sell, others want to buy, and a trade happens where they meet. In decentralized finance there is no order book. Instead, there is a liquidity pool: a smart contract holding two assets, and a formula that sets the price automatically.
lead: Liquidity pools replaced the exchange order book with a mathematical formula. We explain how an automated market maker works, where liquidity providers' returns come from, and what they actually earn their fees for.
author: Editorial

children.value: On a traditional exchange, the price is set by the order book: some want to sell, others want to buy, and a trade happens where they meet. In decentralized finance there is no order book. Instead, there is a
liquidity pool
: a smart contract holding two assets, and a formula that sets the price automatically.
A formula instead of an intermediary
The simplest automated market maker (AMM) keeps the product of the reserves constant:
x · y = k

. If a pool holds 10 ETH and 30,000 USDC, the product equals 300,000. A buyer takes ETH out — there is less of it in the pool, and by the formula each subsequent ETH costs more. The price moves on its own, without a single matching order.
This leads to the key practical consequence:
the larger the pool, the less a trade moves the price

. Swapping $1,000 in a $10M pool is almost imperceptible; the same thousand in a $50,000 pool will shift the price by a percent. This difference between the expected and the actual price is called slippage, and it is precisely its size that distinguishes a "deep" market from an empty one.
What the liquidity provider gets paid for

The assets in a pool are deposited by ordinary users — liquidity providers. Every trade leaves a fee in the pool, usually a fraction of a percent, and it is distributed among all depositors in proportion to their share. It sounds like passive income, but it comes at a cost that gets written in the fine print.
A liquidity provider does not earn the fee "for nothing" — they sell the market a service: the readiness to always be the other side of a trade, including one that is unfavorable to them.

When an asset's price moves away from the entry point, the pool's formula automatically sells the appreciating asset and accumulates the depreciating one. As a result, the value of a share in the pool ends up lower than if the same coins had simply sat in a wallet. This difference is called
impermanent loss
: as long as the price has not returned, the loss is "on paper," but upon exiting the pool it becomes real.
What to check before putting money into a pool
Trading volume relative to pool size.
Fees are paid by trades: a pool with no turnover earns nothing, no matter what APY the calculator draws.
Volatility of the pair.
A pool of two stablecoins barely knows impermanent loss; a pair of a stablecoin and a volatile asset knows it very well.
The source of the advertised yield.
Fees from real trades are the sustainable part; a "rain" of project tokens is a marketing budget that runs out.
Contract audit and protocol age.
The formula is honest, but the code around it is written by people.
Why understand this, even if you don't invest

Liquidity is the load-bearing structure of the entire crypto market. The depth of the pools determines how honest the prices you see in an exchange widget are, how resilient a stablecoin is to panic, and what it actually costs to "cash out" of a position. Any conversation about yield in DeFi is worth starting with the question: where does the liquidity in this scheme come from, and who pays for its presence.
This is educational material, not financial advice.
